He shot part of the movie “osmosis jones” at my sisters house in Plymouth MA where I was staying one summer. I spent a couple weeks seeing him. He was SUPER nice to me and all the neighbors. He was SUPER mean to anyone working on the movie. We’d be playing billiards on the basement & he’d be laughing and asking about our lives and just oozing humble , positive charisma. Then some girl would say “they need you in makeup soon mister Murray”
Bill would start screaming at her and call her fat. Then immediately change course and be back to playing with my kitten.
Very odd dude. He wouldn’t even be mad but would act mad at the staff. 
I once asked him about it… carefully,
He said
“I made a vow to always be mean to assholes and they are all assholes in entertainment.”
I’m paraphrasing but that was the meaning.
I still am on the fence if he’s a good man or scum. The movie people were terrified of him but he adored my drunk idiot brother in law and sent him Xmas presents for years.

author: @leepatterson5710
text:
I've always liked the fact they wake up in the same clothes showing it wasn't about sex, it was about the real connection that was made not just with Mcdowels character, but the town as a whole.  Earlier in the film (IIRC) Murry tries to connect with people thinking it was the key to getting out, but he was doing it for selfish reasons.  By the end he is ultimately changed as a person, perhaps keeping with some original script attempt to speak on Buddhism's ideas of suffering, which I think makes the movie classic.  It isn't just a rom-com, it isn't just sci-fi or philosophical head scratcher.  Groundhog Day is a wonderful mix of all of them by not being too bogged down in anyone of the genres, but being honest and sincere with each them.

One of my 10/10 movies of all time.

replies.author: @jej3451
replies.text: It's not just Buddhism.  People of every religion read their own messages into the movie

replies.author: @leepatterson5710
replies.text: @jej3451 I suppose, I was mainly going off memory of the ending of him sort of accepting suffering at the end, but I'll be honest it has been a few years since I've seen it so it could be based off a wrong memory.

replies.author: @jej3451
replies.text: ​​@leepatterson5710No, your interpretation is entirely valid, but my point was that the movie has become kind of a Rorschach test for people of diverse philosophical outlooks.  Everyone's got their own take on its meaning, and that has been cited as what makes the movie great.

replies.author: @Teddy-lw5lo
replies.text:
@s@stalhandske9649Apparently Ramos intended to shoot the movie in Punxsutawney, but the town wasn’t well suited to shoot a film

Ramis, being from Chicago, knew of Woodstock and elected to transform the town into “Punxsutawney” for filming

Woodstock is very proud of the movie to this day and have celebrated every Groundhog Day since. Thousands of people show up to the square and the theater downtown shows the movie all day long. 

Next February will be Woodstock’s 33rd Groundhog Day, and I recommend you come if you can

author: @criticalmissives9245
text:
99 times out of 100 any of those production issues Drinker mentioned would absolutely torpedo a film. That’s why filmmaking is sometimes as much alchemy as it is art.

A lead and a screenwriter wanting a completely different tone than the director is almost always a death sentence for a movie. That Groundhog Day turned out to be a modern masterpiece just boggles my mind. But it is.

text: One of my all times favorite movies. Not to defend Murray's actions but in a way his insistence on making the script and the movie with a deeper philosophical meaning is what made it so special and truly stand apart. It's just so sad he and Ramis didn't reconcile until short before Ramises passing.

replies.author: @calmbbaer
replies.text: Exactly.  The fact that Ramis's next movie was Stuart Saves His Family says it all... for the few who still remember what that even was.  After that movie, its lead actor's only movie roles were cameos and "as himself," a brutal comedown.  No wonder he went into politics (Franken, not Ramis or Murray).

replies.author: @Osprey850
replies.text: I had that thought while watching, but unless we see the different scripts, we can't know for sure. It's equally possible that Ramis' version did have the light philosophy and meaning that the final movie had and that what Murray wanted was a lot more of that, to the point where the movie would've been more depressing than uplifting (to reflect his feelings over his messy divorce).

replies.author: @brianlagrant
replies.text: @calmbbaer Think what you will, but Ramis actually turned an obscure recurring SNL skit into a pretty good movie.  Stuart Saves His Family is both hilarious and touching.  It might not be as good as Groundhog's Day but it does  convey a deeper, more dramatic themes like alcoholism while still maintaining a comedic tone.  Any other director forgone the touches Ramis makes.  Maybe give it another shot sometime.  Your call...

replies.author: @calmbbaer
replies.text: @brianlagrant - My point wasn't how good I thought it was, but how it did commercially (and, less so, with critics).  Less than a million on a $10 million movie is terrible.  Also, no one forced Ramis to base his next film on an SNL script.  I know it's beloved as a cult film in some (small) circles.  But the give and take of those involved with Groundhog Day helped make it great.

author: @mikemurphy2932
text:
Groundhog day is a perfect example of a film you have to watch more than once.

Roger Ebert gave a positive review in his original column on the film, but in a review in 2005, he noted that its genius was not immediately noticeable.  Another critic noted that Groundhog Day would never be designated a national film treasure by the Library of Congress.  

It was in 2006.

And to think that Bill Murray initially hated the film.

author: @ThePrisoner881
text:
I got to (briefly) meet Murray back in the early 1990's.  I was stationed at 4th MARDIV headquarters in New Orleans.  Me & my buddies were out barhopping, and lo and behold we stumbled across Murray (who was with an unknown Asian woman) at a very late hour in our latest bar.  We were a little drunk (OK, maybe not so little) and I loudly stammered out "Bill!" because I couldn't think of anything else to say.  He turned around and comically said "WHAT???"

It was a funny moment, but only a moment.  He was fooling around at a pool table by himself, his lady friend seemingly not interested.  I offered him a drink and a game of pool on us just as we might any random stranger.  He refused the drink, his demeanor changing to abrasive immediately.  He said "Sure, let's play $1,000 a ball, cash."  It was a blunt way of saying "you're not in my income bracket, so F off."  Being lowly corporals with no real money, we politely declined, at which point Murray took repeated jabs at our economic status (to put it politely).  We left him alone after that.

I get celebrities probably don't always enjoy the attention they get from the public, but we were trying to treat him like a normal person.  No autographs, none of the usual celebrity worship.  I never suspected that side of Murray existed, and I lost a lot of respect for him that night.  There's a nice way to say "thanks but no" and Murray seemed incapable of it.  I doubt Ramis would've reacted the same, and it speaks volumes about the difference between the two.

replies.text:
I once saw Bill Murray at a grocery store in Los Angeles. I told him how cool it was to meet him in person, but I didn’t want to be a douche and bother him and ask him for photos or anything. He said, “Oh, like you’re doing now?” I was taken aback, and all I could say was “Huh?” but he kept cutting me off and going “huh? huh? huh?” and closing his hand shut in front of my face. I walked away and continued with my shopping, and I heard him chuckle as I walked off. When I came to pay for my stuff up front I saw him trying to walk out the doors with like fifteen Milky Ways in his hands without paying.

The girl at the counter was very nice about it and professional, and was like “Sir, you need to pay for those first.” At first he kept pretending to be tired and not hear her, but eventually turned back around and brought them to the counter.

When she took one of the bars and started scanning it multiple times, he stopped her and told her to scan them each individually “to prevent any electrical infetterence,” and then turned around and winked at me. I don’t even think that’s a word. After she scanned each bar and put them in a bag and started to say the price, he kept interrupting her by yawning really loudly.

author: @32FONickweiser
text:
I was a resident of Woodstock, IL for half a decade so seeing you cover this movie for a Production Hell video made my day.

The movie was filmed in the Woodstock Square of the town. I lived a few minutes from there. During my time there, they celebrated Groundhog Day every year at the Square and all the sights you see in the movie are still there from the Gazebo to the restaurants and of course the movie theater. 

Fittingly enough, that exact theater you see plays Groundhog Day every year on February 2nd. 

After watching this video, I had no idea how crazy things got between Bill and Harold. I was aware of Bill being crazy. But I had no clue this was the match that sparked the fire between him and Harold.

Unfortunate considering what they pulled off here was a stellar movie that is getting its proper respect decades later. Who would've thunk it would've spawned one of the all-time great memes?!

With that all said, THANK YOU for doing this video, Drinker!

Former Woodstock Resident,
Nickweiser


P.S. The weather in Illinois is STILL a nightmare to this very day. If you ever hear about Illinois getting the 4 seasons in 1 day, That's not an exaggeration. Our weather is more bipolar than Amber Turd ever was!

author: @kennedysaisurvival
text:
Bill Murray shares rarefied air with Marlon Brando and Val Kilmer in being someone no one wanted/wants to work with and yet has been offered many plum roles in his life. Lost in Translation, Zombieland, What about Bob, The Life Aquatic with Steve Zissou, Ghostbusters, Caddyshack, Stripes, etc., are all movies where you remember vividly that Bill Murray was in that film.

His essential characteristic is being stuck halfway between straight man and slapstick. He knows how to play the kind of dumb where you see it as a defense mechanism for laziness and ego-protection more than as an actual characteristic. Only that essential characteristic can deliver the line "I make it a rule never to get involved with possessed people" and pull it off. I mean, to this day, each time I see that movie, it cracks me up partially because I could actually believe that Venkman could truly have thought about that before the scene and actually have that rule. Still cracks me up.

author: @TheNoonish
text:
Drinker is spot-on about Bill Murray.  I used to think of him as a discerning talent who was picky about what projects he works on (well, this was back before Garfield), but the truth is that he's just a major prima-donna pain in the ass, and thinks he's smarter than everyone else.  That kind of energy is useful for some of his iconic roles, like Ghostbusters, but it doesn't make him great.

As for the movie's overall tone, I prefer the more dramatic and serious exploration of the situation. Phil has to undergo character growth in a static world.  He starts by seeking help, trying to "fix" whatever's wrong, but that doesn't work.  Then he moves into a type of hedonism, but finds that unfulfilling.  He gets fixated on Rita, but he's still too self-centered to make an actual connection with him.  That moves him into depression and nihilism, where he's tired himself out of doing literally everything there is to do and accepts his own death, only to find he can't even die.  Which then leads him to finally make an ACTUAL connection with Rita, and he discovers that the end of the cycle of immortality is altruism.   It's only at that point the cycle breaks, when he's sufficiently changed as a character to no longer be the same man who was initially trapped in the loop.  The romance is fairly incidental-it's a motivating factor, but it required Phil to become a better man, which is ultimately what seems to break the cycle.

So in the end, I don't think Ramis' idea of making it a romantic comedy with a cooky premise was wise.  I think the final product ends up working well because the comedic beats lighten what would be a very dour and dark story without them, but I really don't view this film as a comedy overall.  It's a feel-good drama about life, with some romance and humor mixed in.

author: @Goshdarnet
text:
Bill Murray is the reason legendary voice actor Lawrence Music lost his job.

When The Real Ghostbusters cartoon came out in the late 80s, Lawrence Music voiced Peter Venkman.  Lawrence Music is also the long time voice of Garfield in the cartoon specials and series cartoon.  Bill didn't like that yhe cartoon version of Peter sounded like Garfield and he had Music fired, replaced by Dave Coulier (Joey from Full House).

And of course, irony of ironies, when they tried to bring Garfield back in movies decades later, Murray had no problem taking on the character of Garfield himself, after Lawrence Music had passed away.

text:
Bill Murray's flaws are forgiven because he's Bill Murray has a very good reason. The very people who found him difficult were themselves later described as difficult people to work with. Also there are so many stories of thoughtful and selfless acts on his part throughout the years. What is noted about him more than anything is his kinder treatment of those who have less power and authority on filmsets. Often acting in defense of those who are treated as unimportant and bullied. 
Add to that his own admission of regret for a bad attitude on certain projects in the past. The guy is pretty self aware and not all that bad at his core.
